title: Intro
text: Welcome to Science Radar, your weekly digest of the most surprising, frontier-pushing, and cross-disciplinary discoveries from across all of science. This week we've found papers that challenge long-held assumptions, open doors to entirely new research directions, and connect ideas across fields in ways nobody expected. Let's dive in.

title: This Week's Highlights
text: And now, a quick tour through this week's other noteworthy papers.

title: Psilocin fosters neuroplasticity in iPSC-derived human cortical neurons
text: Psilocin fosters neuroplasticity in iPSC-derived human cortical neurons — eLife. Psilocybin is studied as innovative medication in anxiety, substance abuse and treatment-resistant depression. title: Death receptor 6 does not regulate axon degeneration and Schwann cell injury responses during Wallerian degeneration
text: Death receptor 6 does not regulate axon degeneration and Schwann cell injury responses during Wallerian degeneration — eLife. Axon degeneration (AxD), accompanied by glial remodeling, is a pathological hallmark of many neurodegenerative diseases, leading to the disruption of neuronal connectivity. Understanding the mechanisms in neurons and glia that regulate AxD is essential for developing therapeutic strategies to preven

title: Human exploration strategically balances approaching and avoiding uncertainty
text: Human exploration strategically balances approaching and avoiding uncertainty — eLife. A central purpose of exploration is to reduce goal-relevant uncertainty. Consequently, individuals often explore by focusing on areas of uncertainty in the environment. However, people sometimes adopt the opposite strategy, one of avoiding uncertainty. How are the conflicting tendencies to approach

title: Nociceptor neurons control pollution-mediated neutrophilic asthma
text: Nociceptor neurons control pollution-mediated neutrophilic asthma — eLife. The immune and sensory nervous systems, having evolved in parallel, communicate through shared receptors and transmitters to maintain homeostasis and respond to both external and internal disruptions. Although neural responses often confer protective benefits, they can also exacerbate inflammation d

title: Outro
text: That's Science Radar for this week. Every one of these papers is a reminder that the most interesting science often lives at the edges — where fields collide, where assumptions break, and where the next big question is just starting to take shape. Until next week, keep questioning everything.
